text: Single port laparoscopic heartman reversal. This is the case of a fifty nine year old man who underwent a laparotomic heartmans procedure because of peritonitis due to a complicated diverticular disease in another hospital. Nine months after this procedure a laparoscopic heartman reversal using the single port technique was planned in our institution.

The surgeon excises and mobilizes the colostomy, detaching it from the abdominal wall. The anvil of the circular stapler is introduced and secured in the colon using a hand-sewn pursing suture. The colon is then repositioned into the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon utilizes the colostomy site as an access point for the single port device, inserting one trocar for the camera and two working trocars. Pneumoperitoneum is established at a pressure of twelve millimeters of mercury, and standard laparoscopic instruments along with an intracorporeal articulating sealing device are employed.

The surgeon, positioned between the patient's legs, performs adhesiolysis with the patient in a right side tilt. The descending colon and splenic flexure are mobilized from lateral to medial, enhancing the mobility of the left colon. Monitors are centrally placed for optimal visualization.
The surgeon, assisted from the right side of the patient, detaches loops of the small bowel from the pelvis to uncage the rectal sigmoidal stump. The stump is then mobilized and isolated.

The surgeon advances the circular stapler into the rectosigmoidal stump and connects it with the anvil to create a tension-free anastomosis. The integrity of the anastomosis is then tested using saline and air.
The surgeon uses absorbable sutures to close the ostomy site, followed by closing the skin with a pursed ring suture using non-absorbable material.
